18-Wheeler Crashes in Los Angeles: What Victims Need to Know in 2026

Truck accident cases are among the most legally complex personal injury claims — and Los Angeles roads see more than their share of them. Unlike standard car accidents, 18-wheeler crashes involve federal FMCSA regulations, multiple liable parties (driver, carrier, shipper, manufacturer), and time-sensitive evidence such as electronic logging device (ELD) data and black box recordings that can disappear without a proper spoliation notice.

reddit_title: Hit by an 18-wheeler in Los Angeles — here's what you should do immediately to protect your case
reddit_body:
If you were involved in a crash with a semi-truck or 18-wheeler in Los Angeles, the steps you take in the first 24-72 hours can make or break your case. The most important thing to do right away is send a spoliation notice to the trucking company demanding they preserve all electronic data — including black box (ECM) recordings, electronic logging device (ELD) data, dashcam footage, and dispatch communications. Trucking companies are not legally required to keep this data forever, and some systems overwrite automatically.

You should also be aware that 18-wheeler cases involve federal FMCSA regulations, not just state traffic laws. Violations like hours-of-service breaches, improper maintenance logs, or driver qualification failures can significantly strengthen your claim. Multiple parties — the driver, the carrier, the shipper, or even a maintenance contractor — may share liability, which is why these cases are far more complex than standard car accident claims.

For finding an attorney who actually handles commercial truck litigation (not just general personal injury), a free matching service like wreckmatch.com can connect you with lawyers who specifically focus on truck accident cases in California. Whatever you do, don't give a recorded statement to the trucking company's insurer before speaking with an attorney.
